data.title: Global IPO Activity Picking Up But Continues To Lag Other Post-COVID Years
data.description:
Global IPO activity is slowly recovering from the lows of early this year, but transactions are well behind the pace of recent years.

There were 326 IPOs worldwide in the third quarter, the most since the fourth quarter of 2023. However, those offerings only came to an aggregate of $23.32 billion, the lowest since the first quarter of 2019, according to the latest S&P Global Market Intelligence data. Through the first three quarters of 2024, there were 946 IPOs launched with $76.44 billion offered, below the first three quarters of 2023 when 1,061 IPOs were launched offering an aggregate $95.76 billion.

These numbers are well below the peak of IPO activity in 2021 when, through the first three quarters of that year, 2,355 IPOs were launched offering an aggregate $471.05 billion.

In the US, activity has risen steadily since the end of 2023, with 36 IPOs launched in the third quarter, up from 35 in the second quarter and 25 in the first.

There have been 347 IPOs since the start of 2022, which is nine fewer than the number of IPOs launched in the first quarter of 2021 alone.

There has been $26.28 billion offered through US IPOs in the first three quarters of 2024, nearly double the $13.73 billion offered through the first three quarters of 2023.

The largest IPO in the US so far in 2024 was for Lineage Inc. (LINE), a real estate investment trust for temperature-controlled warehouses, which offered $5.10 billion in July.

European IPO activity slowed in the third quarter, with just 30 transactions and $770 million offered, compared to 47 IPOs launched and $10.33 billion offered in the same quarter last year. Through the first three quarters of 2024 there were 98 IPOs launched in Europe, compared to 111 IPOs in the first three quarters of 2023.

There has been $17.46 billion offered through European IPOs in the first three quarters of this year, compared to $13.50 billion through the third quarter of 2023.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arch Capital: Navigating Insurance Cycles With Strong Pricing Power
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Pythia Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Arch Capital Group Ltd. (NASDAQ:ACGL) represents a compelling investment case, as the diversified business model-insurance, reinsurance, and mortgage insurance-lets multichannel revenues arise while the risk profile is well-balanced. Moreover, the portfolio of investments is well-positioned in case of a slide in interest rates, and the mortgage book assures low credit risk since the focus is on high-credit borrowers.

Although interest rate sensitivity, credit exposure, and regulatory challenges are potential headwinds, disciplined underwriting at Arch-Office, strategic acquisitions, and sound capital management practices have positioned it favorably to deal with market uncertainties for long-term growth.

The recent hurricanes in Florida impacted market psychology around ACGL, with solid underwriting and catastrophe management boosting investor confidence, reflected in its 50% YTD rise. However, heightened exposure to catastrophe-prone regions may increase fear among investors, worrying about rising claims and volatility. While ACGL's risk management mitigates concerns, sentiment remains a balance between overconfidence in short-term success and caution over potential long-term risks.

Arch Capital Poised For Growth Amid A Dynamic P&C Market Cycle

Property and casualty insurance, reinsurance, and mortgage insurance are provided by Arch Capital Group Ltd. The present phase of the insurance cycle is hardening, marked by higher prices with surging demand because natural catastrophes reached a record high of $151 billion. The conditions are right for Arch to flex its pricing muscles and maintain good underwriting performance.

However, Arch Capital Group's second-quarter performance in its P&C segments reflects the strength of its leadership and strategic underwriting. Despite a higher frequency of catastrophic events, the Reinsurance segment generated $366 million of underwriting income. To manage heightened storm risk, Arch avoided expanding its property catastrophe exposure during midyear renewals, emphasizing portfolio balance. Catastrophe losses were $196 million, with 70% from U.S. secondary perils.

Additionally, the Probable Maximum Loss (PML) represents the maximum loss Arch Capital could face from a single catastrophic event, calculated to a specific confidence level (in this case, a one-in-250-year event). Arch's PML is 7.9% of its tangible shareholders' equity, a clear indication that they have assessed and limited its risk exposure to stay well within its internal risk limits. This active management of catastrophe risk ensures that Arch Capital remains secure, even in extreme scenarios.

On the positive side, the US P&C insurance industry posted its best first-quarter underwriting result in more than 15 years during 2024, underpinning strong premium growth with softening claims cost inflation. Its combined ratio was 94%, while the Return on Equity (ROE) stood at 14%. The outlook for the remainder of 2024 and through 2025 is positive, with forecasted ROEs of 9.5% in 2024 and 10.0% in 2025.

Additionally, the US P&C insurance market is likely to grow at a CAGR of 6.4% from 2024 to 2030, driven by technological migration like AI and machine learning. This technology is being implemented as insurers arm themselves with better risk assessment, underwriting, and claims management, leading to more accurate pricing models and generally a smoother process. For Arch Capital, this positive outlook, along with its focus on data-driven practices, positions it well to capitalize on market opportunities in the evolving P&C landscape.

Furthermore, higher interest rates may continue to lower the number of newly originated mortgages in the Mortgage segment. On the other hand, continued stable demand for housing, along with conservative underwriting practices adopted by Arch Capital, has set a very good backdrop for the continued growth of this segment. However, beyond 2025, the property casualty insurance industry will continue to be dynamic, driven by changes in climate, technology, and marketplace regulation.

High mortgage rates have cooled new home sales, preventing many would-be buyers and further depressing new mortgage originations. This presents both an environment of challenges and opportunities for Arch Capital. While suppressed mortgage origination will likely limit growth in the mortgage insurance segment, the sustained demand for housing amid high borrowing costs puts a cushion under the lender's portfolio of mortgages outstanding.

The affordability index reached its peak in early 2024, reflecting higher levels of affordability, then decreased through mid-2024. Usually, a lower affordability index means fewer new mortgage originations for Arch Capital, operating in the mortgage insurance market because potential buyers need more money. This limits growth in Arch's mortgage insurance segment.

Arch's conservative underwriting practices, focused on high-quality borrowers who thus normally have better credit scores, would also help rein in the potential risk of default in a much tighter lending environment. A higher interest rate environment could also lead to more homeowners holding on to their existing mortgages, reducing refinance activity but perhaps stabilizing Arch's insured portfolio.

Looking ahead, with high interest rates and inventory shortages combined to create headwinds in the market as a whole, the underpinning of Arch Capital-through its risk management approach and focus on quality borrowers-has positioned it to weather those headwinds. But if these rates stay high for longer, this is consistent with the slowing growth of new mortgage insurance, adding pressure to affordability and overall market dynamics.

Navigating Through Risk With A Well-Diversified Portfolio

The portfolio of Arch Capital runs from insurance to reinsurance to mortgage insurance, with gross premiums amounting to $2.1 billion, $2.9 billion, and $340 million in the second quarter of 2024, respectively. Included in the insurance segment are specialty product lines, such as property and casualty, that reach out to special niche markets so that the profitability of the same may remain intact in the wake of natural and economic perils.

Meanwhile, its reinsurance arm has capacity across various risks and boasts a strong combined ratio of 79.5% in Q2 2024, indicative of excellent claims and cost management. Most of the mortgage insurance business is domiciled in the United States, underwriting homebuyers with data-driven, forward-thinking risk assessments- such as FICO scores- to keep it one step ahead of housing market trends and manage the potential for defaults. Through portfolio diversification in these segments, Arch Capital effectively balances growth with the mitigation of risk, thus guaranteeing stability in earnings amidst the most dynamic market conditions.

Income Conservative Investment Strategy

The investment portfolio of Arch Capital, valued at the market value of $37.1 billion as of June 2024, is conservatively positioned, with the primary objective being capital preservation and maintenance of liquidity. Fixed maturities represent the largest-capitalized class of investments, represented via an accounting value of $25.2 billion for corporate bonds and government securities. Unrealized losses have occurred due to rising interest rates; the high credit quality of the portfolio somewhat mitigates downside risks.

The company's net investment income increased to $364 million in Q2 2024, compared to $242 million the prior year, reflecting strategic asset allocation and strong interest rates. Furthermore, the Arch balance sheet equity portfolio has a book value of $1.4 billion and a balanced mixture of income and appreciation. This conservative underpinning, but active management investment strategy tends to make earnings predictable, continuing to nurture capital strength in turbulent market conditions.

Notably, Arch Capital's investment portfolio shows strong credit quality, with an average credit rating of "AA-" from S&P and "Aa3" from Moody's. The benchmark return index used to compare performance has an average credit quality of "A1" by Moody's, indicating a slightly lower credit risk compared to Arch's portfolio. For the six months ending June 30, 2024, Arch's total return on investments was 2.14%, surpassing the benchmark's 2.07%. The company maintains an investment duration of 2.83 years, which helps manage interest rate risk while focusing on high-quality assets. Therefore, this conservative investment approach contributes to steady returns and limits exposure to credit risk.

Active Credit Analysis And Risk Management Success

Arch Capital's strength partly comes from its stringent credit risk management, particularly within its mortgage insurance and reinsurance operations. Using borrower credit scores, such as FICO, and the loan-to-value ratio helps the company measure the mortgage risks of default, limiting the number of probable claims and losses. The company maintains an allowance for credit loss to protect against defaults in payment, which currently stands at $36 million as of June 2024.

Favorably, the new mortgage insurance written by Arch Capital in the first half of 2024 continues to exhibit high credit quality. Approximately 69.5% of newly originated loans had FICO scores of 740 or better, while the proportion for the first half of 2023 was 65.3%. The volume with FICO scores ranging between 680 and 739 constituted 27.2%. Still, low-risk scores less than 620 constituted an almost zero share of the portfolio, hence emphasizing conservative credit underwriting performed by Arch.

From the analysis of the Loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, it can be observed that most loans have concentrated in a bracket of 90.01% to 95%, accounting for about 53.9% in total. Loans with LTVs over 95.01% account for only 6.7%, which illustrates that Arch Capital pays close attention to credit risk by underwriting loans with a relatively strong equity position on the borrower's part.

This strategic positioning within the mortgage insurance portfolio underlines Arch's commitment to a high-quality business book, reducing potential default risks while reinforcing its overall financial stability.

Arch Capital's mortgage insurance business is also seeing an uptick in delinquent loans, from 18,286 in 2023 to 20,422 in 2024. The rate has increased in 2024 to 1.82%, compared to 1.61% in 2023, and reflects a small uptick in defaults. The firm has been quick, however, with 21,423 loans "curing"-that is, being resolved-outpacing the 20,434 new notices of delinquency this year.

Further, Arch paid 571 claims in 2024 for $18.3 million, at an average cost per claim of $32,100. However, the severity rate of claims has slightly decreased to 67.8% in 2024 from 71.5% in 2023, indicating generally that the loss environment is controlled. The average case reserve per default has dropped to $17,100 in 2024 from $23,100 in 2023, showing the effectiveness of loss reserves managed by Arch.

Although the delinquency ratio slightly trends upward, the high concentration that Arch Capital places on credit quality-evidenced by the high FICO scores and the efficiency in the handling of defaults via cures and appropriate reserves, underpin a good credit risk management strategy and further strengthen the stability of the company in the mortgage insurance market.

Backstopping the process, Arch reinsures $7.47 billion, 66.5% from carriers rated "A-" or better by A.M. Best, to reduce risk against counterparty exposure further. In addition, Arch continued to report net favorable development on prior-year loss reserves estimated at $121 million in Q2 2024, reflecting the conserving nature of the reserves managed. Added to these measures is the active monitoring of the claims trend, which protects the company's capital base against eventual losses.

Interest Rate Sensitivity: Rate Drops Could Boost Arch Capital's Portfolio

If the Federal Reserve continues to lower the interest rates, Arch Capital Group Ltd. stands to gain in several ways. If interest rates decrease by 50 basis points (0.5%), the value of Arch Capital's fixed-income portfolio would increase by about $378 million. This is because bond prices rise when interest rates fall.

To keep it simple, the portfolio's duration is 3 years, and we multiply this by the 0.5% rate decrease and the portfolio size ($25.2 billion). The calculation is 3×0.5%×25.2 billion=378 million3×0.5%×25.2 billion=$378 million. While this gain boosts the portfolio's value in the short term, it also means lower yields on future investments, potentially reducing income. Conversely, if rates go up by 0.5%, the portfolio would lose about $378 million in value.

Of course, offsetting this is that lower interest rates come with lower yields on new investments, which could suppress investment income over time. This, in turn, could compress margins from the insurance business due to enhanced competition and price pressures. Like Arch Capital, a rate decline could have some initial favorable effects, positive in the short run, through portfolio appreciation and upward volume in mortgage insurance. Over time, however, any likely related decline in investment income may be difficult to overcome. Thus, what a sustained low-rate environment is may turn into a mixed bag wherein portfolio gains offset pressure on earnings.

Finally, Arch Capital's portfolio has a relatively high average yield of ~5.8% (calculated by taking the annualized net investment income of $1.456 billion and dividing it by the portfolio's total value of $25.2 billion), which benefits when interest rates decline. This decline boosts the market value of Arch's bonds since they offer a better return than newly issued bonds, leading to unrealized gains and potentially enhancing the company's balance sheet.

Valuation Signals Strength With A Cautious Outlook

Arch Capital's valuation metrics indicate a blend of market optimism and caution. The declining forward P/E ratios from 13.46 in 2023 to 11.78 by 2026 reflect expectations of moderate earnings growth, with consensus estimates projecting an EPS growth rate of 7% in 2024, slowing to 0.6% in 2025, and rebounding to 6% in 2026. These figures suggest that while Arch is expected to continue growing, the pace may be modest.

The company's Price-to-Book (P/B) ratio of 2.14 - expected to drop slightly to 2.00 - is notably higher than the sector median of 1.22, indicating a 76% premium over its peers. This premium suggests the market values Arch's equity highly, likely due to its sound risk management, consistent earnings, and diversified portfolio. However, this also means investors are paying a premium for stability, indicating confidence but also caution, as the stock might be seen as relatively expensive compared to the sector.

From a peer comparative perspective, ACGL offers an attractive valuation compared to its peers, with a P/E Non-GAAP (FY1) of 12.11, making it competitively priced against Hartford Financial Services (HIG) at 11.75 and significantly better than Intact Financial (OTCPK:IFCZF) at 19.18. Its forward P/E ratios of 11.88 (FY2) and 11.29 (FY3) reflect consistent value, while its PEG ratio of 2.03 suggests solid growth potential. Despite a slightly higher price-to-growth ratio than HIG, ACGL outpaces peers like Allstate (ALL), positioning itself as a strong growth candidate.

Moreover, ACGL's Price-to-Cash Flow (TTM) of 6.19 is more attractive than Fairfax Financial's of 9.14, highlighting more robust cash flow returns. Additionally, its P/B ratio of 2.10, at a slight premium, reflects market confidence in its profitability.

Overall, the P/E ratios suggest that Arch is reasonably priced with room for growth, while the P/B ratio implies a market willingness to pay for Arch's financial stability and robust performance. The valuation portrays Arch as a strong player in the industry, yet it also underscores the importance of managing expectations, given its premium pricing.

Risk Assessment: Economic And Regulatory Challenges

Other key risks keeping Arch Capital Group on its toes relate, in particular, to interest rates - and thus to the regulatory environment. The bottom line is that the Federal Reserve's ongoing decreases in interest rates may lead to lower investment income owing to lower yields from new fixed-income investments. Lower rates would favor housing demand and reduce mortgage defaults, but they would also compress the profitability in the mortgage insurance segment due to increasing competition and price pressures. Further complicating matters is the regulatory framework.

New regulations, such as the Private Mortgage Insurer Eligibility Requirements PMIERs-imposed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, detail minimum levels of capital and operations that a mortgage insurer should have on its books. Any adjustment to PMIERs based on economic performance or changes in the housing market would result in changes to Arch's underwriting policy or capital reserves and capacity to underwrite new business. Another reason may be the continuously changing legislation concerning climate-related issues in the insurance industry, which could affect property and casualty reinsurance in adapting to the risk modeling and deployment of capital.

Bottom Line

In summary, Arch Capital is a well-diversified and financially robust company, offering strong cash flows, effective risk management, and upside potential, particularly in a declining interest rate environment. Its current valuation, combined with solid fundamentals, makes ACGL an attractive long-term investment in the insurance sector.

data.title: Citigroup: Great Quarter Ignored
data.description:
For a stock trading below tangible book value, Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C) doesn't need great results to warrant a higher stock price. The large bank reported another strong quarter, yet the stock slumped 5% on regulator related concerns. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish with dips providing a great opportunity to own Citigroup.

Another Good Quarter

Citigroup soared past analyst estimates, posting the following Q3'24 results:

Despite the solid results, the stock slumped during the day. Citigroup had higher credit costs and discussed problems with finding employees for remediation services, but the real issue was likely the bizarre questions regarding an asset cap.

On the Q3'24 earnings call, several analysts asked by an asset cap, with CEO Jane Fraser clearly answering the question:

So let me be crystal clear. We do not have an asset cap and there are no additional measures other than what was announced in July in place and not expecting any. So then the implications of what we're doing as I've laid out, we've increased investments in the areas where we were behind, particularly in the data related to our regulatory processes and regulatory reporting. We're increasing investment behind it, and we continue to make progress -- material progress on the orders in place, including closing the BSA/AML order this quarter. The third order closed since 2021.

The questions stem from Citigroup getting hit with fines of $136 million back in July by the Federal Reserve Board and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency related to the 2020 consent orders. Citigroup has made substantial progress in the years, but the bank is still struggling with data quality provided to the regulators.

Regardless, Citigroup doesn't appear in any scenario where the bank would be hit with an asset cap. In the last quarter, the large bank boosted tangible book value and the CET1 capital ratio during the quarter.

Citigroup spent a large $2.7 billion on credit costs while still generating $3.0 billion in Q3 net income, leading to the $1.51 EPS. The credit costs were even up 45% from last September quarter levels of $1.8 billion, but the key is that $0.5 billion of the credit costs were for building the allowance for credit losses reserves.

The large bank now has $22 billion in total reserves for a reserve-to-funded loans ratio of ~2.7%. One really has to question whether Citigroup needed to build the loan reserves to over 10 quarters of the Q3'24 net-charge off level of ~$2.2 billion.

Even with the excess ACL build, Citigroup still built the TBV up to $89.67. The stock ended the day trading at only $62, or at only 0.7x TBV.

The large bank still needs to improve returns, but the discount to other banks remains excessive. Wells Fargo (WFC) still has an asset cap, yet the stock has rebounded to trade at double the P/TBV at 1.6x. Bank of America (BAC) trades at a similar valuation of 1.6x TBV, while JPMorgan Chase (JPM) trades at the premium of the group at 2.4.x

Citigroup still has a rather weak RoTCE of only 7.0%. The large bank needs to improve this metric in order to move back up to the valuations of the large bank group.

CEO Jane Fraser continues to reiterate the goal of reaching an 11.5% RoTCE target. The large bank is forecasting a big boost in returns, which means a big EPS boost.

Citigroup expects to strip out several billion in annual employee costs, with a goal of the efficiency ratio ultimately dipping below 60%. The forecast is for operating expenses to dip to ~$52 billion while revenue grow around $5 billion into 2026.

The consensus prediction by analysts is for Citigroup to earn nearly $8.50 per share in 2026, up from $5.73 this year. The bank has substantial upside via boosting the returns.

Excess Capital

Citigroup returned $2.1 billion to shareholders, yet still slightly increased the CET1 ratio to 13.7%. The large bank saw the regulatory capital ratio dip to only 12.1%, leaving a solid cushion after the 100 basis points management buffer.

The bank offers a very solid 3.4% dividend yield now and spent $1.0 billion on share buybacks attractively priced below TBV. The big question is how much Basel III will impact the future credit ratios, with signs the regulatory push is slipping. Citigroup should be able to continue similar quarterly capital returns while still boosting capital ratios, suggesting some increases in share buybacks are possible.

Previously, Citigroup was a stock with a logical path to $100 based on the full earnings potential of the firm. At this point, the large bank will report a TBV of $100 by the time the stock reaches that level, with nearly $3 in gains to the book value metric in the last year.

Under this scenario, Citigroup would have to eventually trade at $150+ to trade at a similar P/TBV as peers in the large banking sector. The bank has a long way to go to achieve the RoTCE targets to warrant this higher valuation multiple, and investors aren't guaranteed of success.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is the Citigroup slipped after a solid quarter due to some unexpected questions on an unlikely asset cap imposed by regulators. Investors should use any weakness in the stock to continue loading up on Citigroup, trading far below TBV while working on a path to higher returns.

data.title: Southwest Gas: Dividend Aristocrat With Big Upside Potential
data.description:
Southwest Gas (NYSE:SWX) is a rapidly growing natural gas utility operating in Arizona, Nevada and California. The company's sizeable investments in recent years have taken a toll on reported earnings, resulting in a weak stock price performance.

As the pace of capital investments slows, the profitability of Southwest Gas will recover, we expect the earnings of the business to double over the next five years, leading to substantial equity value gains. Southwest also currently pays a 3.3% dividend yield.

Utilities are favoured by income-seeking investors with low-risk tolerances. Despite the resilient nature of Southwest Gas earnings, it also offers appealing growth prospects.

Utilities do not go on sale very often due to the predictable nature of earnings; however, Southwest is a rather unique case as operating performance has been obscured by elevated capital spending as well as some corporate governance and regulatory issues.

Most importantly, Southwest is a regulated utility and the core assets generate stable and predictable returns. Despite some issues, the risk of capital loss is very low with this one.

Southwest Gas today does look somewhat rough, contributing to the attractive valuation, but we believe that the business will start reporting significantly better numbers in a few years. The earnings will grow and so will valuation.

Capital investment program depressed profitability

Southwest Gas has gone through a period of particularly rapid growth, the operating asset base increased 65% over the last five years and is set to rise further as new pipelines are brought on-stream and start generating revenues.

Regulated utilities need to build the infrastructure first and only then can start generating revenues from their investments. During a period of rapid expansion, cash is being spent and pipelines are built, but they generate no revenues. These periods of rapid expansion are associated with declining returns on capital for a utility as a share of temporarily unproductive investments increases. On top of this, large investment programs require planning and coordination and therefore operating costs increase.

As the capital investment program accelerated, the profit margin as well as the returns on capital investment have plummeted, even though the returns allowed by regulators have stayed broadly flat. Accelerating inflation has also played a part in profitability reduction.

Despite the rapid investment and asset base growth, the share price of Southwest has declined as earnings were reduced.

But profitability will recover…

The underlying regulated returns have not changed, the timing is the issue.

As new rate cases are being filed, the capital investments already made will be added to the rate base and will start generating revenues. At the same time, part of the inflation-related operating cost increases will also be passed on. Revenues will increase considerably, and the profit margins will recover.

Southwest Gas expects to grow earnings significantly faster than the asset base over the next 3 years as higher gas rates are being approved by state regulators. SWX expects to generate close to $300 million of earnings by FY2026.

I believe there is a good chance that the company will beat this earning guidance and will also continue growing earnings rapidly beyond the initial forecast period ending FY2026.

The capital spending is expected to remain at an elevated level of about ~$800+ million per annum, over the next three years, twice the maintenance level. Ongoing rapid capital spending will continue putting pressure on reported earnings over the next 3 years, but the underlying value will continue to increase rapidly.

As reported earnings will continue being inconsistent with the underlying value, due to the rapid growth, it is quite useful to adopt an asset-based valuation for Southwest Gas. Southwest is a regulated utility, and therefore their asset values are quite predictable.

The underlying value of Southwest Gas

Southwest Gas is a regulated utility and is allowed to earn a pre-determined rate of return on capital invested to build out the gas network. Most regulated gas operators are allowed to generate roughly 9.5% return on equity.

At the end of this financial year, Southwest will have about $6.4 billion operating rate base approved by regulators, which will continue growing by about 7% per annum as new assets are commissioned.

In a stable operating environment, Southwest should also be able to generate those ~9.5% on capital investments on average, and some of their older investments are indeed generating this return. The problem is that the new investments are obscuring the operating performance of these.

With a $6.4 billion rate base and notional allowed leverage of 50%, Southwest has $3.2 billion of equity invested in its regulated assets. The rest is financed by debt and the cost is passed on.

The $3.2 billion of equity should be able to generate $300 million of earnings. This is ~$60 million less than the company's earnings guidance for FY2024.

US Gas Utilities are trading at an average earnings multiple of 18x, and therefore we can estimate that the Southwest regulated assets should be worth about ~$5.4 billion currently.

On top of this, we would have to include pipelines that have already been built to this date but have not yet been approved by regulators and on-streamed. Our initial estimates suggest a potential value of another $1.5 billion or so. The regulated assets could thus be worth as much as $6.7 billion.

Southwest is also continuing to invest aggressively and grow its asset base. At a growth rate of 6%, as guided by the business, regulated assets could reach ~$9 billion in five years.

Valuation

Southwest Gas owns a regulated gas distribution business that is currently worth over $5.4 billion, and it also has a majority of investment in publicly listed Centuri Holdings (CTRI) worth about $1.2 billion.

Southwest Gas currently has more than $6.4 billion of assets, and it currently trades for $5.3 billion. There is at least a 20% upside to the stock price currently.

On top of this, Southwest is continuing to invest and grow its regulated asset base. Over the next 3 years, the value will grow by ~7% per annum on average. I do believe that this guidance is somewhat conservative given the high level of investment that has been undertaken.

Southwest is a growing regulated utility that can be caught at a discount to the underlying value of its assets.

SWX shareholders will benefit from the gradual value growth as well as the dividend income, which should add up to 10.5% per annum on average.

On top of this, as the growth of business slows, reported earnings will recover, which will lead to substantial earnings and valuation improvement. We estimate that the Southwest-regulated earnings could double over the next 5 years.

Assuming that the regulated asset base reaches ~$9 billion in five years, Southwest could then be allowed to generate $425 of regulated equity employed. Last year, the business generated only ~$220 million of regulated earnings.

The stock is currently trading at 26X forward earnings, reflecting some of this growth potential. On the other hand, there is still plenty of upside.

Risks

Regulation can make or break utility investments. The Southwest is not particularly diversified, with the majority of assets located in only two states, Arizona and Nevada.

Arizona has been outlined by industry observers as having a rather adverse regulatory climate.

Regulators might make life difficult for utility companies, especially when the network expands and the added cost has to be passed on via higher rates. There has been some pushback in Arizona.

The Summary

  1. Southwest Gas has been growing rapidly, and its profitability has declined as a result.
  2. Once growth slows and all the assets start generating revenues, the profitability of the business will improve substantially.
  3. Southwest is currently selling at a 20%+ discount to the value of its assets, we can therefore buy this growing business at a discount.
  4. Growth will continue for the next few years, driving further value growth.

Side note:

The best investment ideas are mostly found off the beaten path. They are also the most difficult to monetise. This article will probably be read by a few hundred people only. I would be grateful if you could comment to keep the show going.

data.title: Suncor Energy: The Best Is Yet To Come
data.description:
Article Thesis

Suncor Energy Inc. (NYSE:SU) is a high-quality Canadian oil company that generates strong results and that should be able to increase its shareholder returns substantially in the foreseeable future as its debt levels decline. A nice starting yield and an undemanding valuation round out the positive picture.

Past Coverage

I have written about Suncor Energy Inc. a couple of times here on Seeking Alpha, most recently in July, a little more than four months ago. I gave the company a "Buy" rating back then, with Suncor Energy returning around 3% since then. With the company releasing its most recent earnings results since my last article, and with some important macro developments since then, it is time for an update.

Oil Price Macro: Opportunities And Threats

So far this year, oil prices have been volatile, seeing some ups and downs, as we can see in the following chart:

Oil prices are currently higher than they were at the beginning of the year, and oil prices are substantially higher compared to early September, but oil nevertheless trades below the highs seen in spring. A couple of macro developments are noteworthy:

- China's economic growth remains unappealing for now, as the country continues to struggle with a real estate downturn. Overall consumption in China is rather weak as consumers are conservative with their cash due to uncertainties, which also impacts oil demand. The IEA states that oil consumption in China is down. On the other hand, China has recently indicated that there will be increased economic stimulus in the foreseeable future, which may result in improving oil demand in the coming quarters. So while China's rather weak economic growth today (relative to the country's growth in the past) is a drag on oil prices, there is potential for improvement with increased stimulus spending.

- Conflict in the Middle East has escalated recently, with Iran attacking Israel with around 200 missiles. Israel will very likely strike back in some way, possibly attacking Iran's oil infrastructure. This could result in lower supply from Iran in the future, which is why oil prices firmed as tensions in the Middle East rose.

- Electric vehicle enthusiasm seems to wane in some countries, especially in Europe, where EV sales have declined in the recent past. It looks like some projections about the growth of electric vehicles were overly optimistic. This means that more ICE vehicles will be sold, compared to a scenario of fast EV growth, which should result in higher oil consumption down the road. This does not have an immediate effect, but if consumers are less likely to switch to EVs going forward, that should be a positive for oil demand in the long run.

- There is a risk of an economic downturn and recession in the US. While this is far from guaranteed, Goldman Sachs (GS) sees a 20% recession probability over the next year, for example. If a recession were to happen, consumers would likely travel less, and economic activity would decline, which would result in lower oil demand, thereby likely causing a decline in oil prices.

Overall, there are thus macro factors that could result in higher oil prices going forward, but there are also potential "threats" for oil prices to keep an eye on. I am relatively neutral on oil markets right now and believe that Suncor Energy is relatively well positioned, no matter what due to being a low-cost producer -- after all, the company even generated positive operating cash flows in the ultra-weak oil price environment in 2020 when the world ground to a halt.

Suncor: Operationally Strong, Deleveraging At A Nice Pace

The macro picture can be important, but so is a company's operational performance. In the most recent quarter, Suncor Energy did pretty well: The company beat estimates on both lines, as analysts once again underestimated the Canadian energy giant.

Delving into the details, we see that Suncor Energy's production averaged 771,000 barrels per day in Q2, which was up nicely versus the previous year's quarter, during which Suncor had produced around 742,000 barrels daily. This makes for a growth rate of 4%, which is a compelling result. Production levels were down slightly compared to the first quarter of the current year, but this was to be expected due to turnaround activity at Suncor Energy's Oil Sands Base bitumen asset. Meanwhile, production of synthetic crude oil was down slightly as well, with planned maintenance being the key factor. With these headwinds having passed now, Suncor Energy is positioned to deliver even better production numbers during the second half of the year. Production should be up nicely thanks to the company's increased interest in the Fort Hills oil sands asset and due to strong production levels at Firebag. Overall, Suncor Energy plans to grow production by around 100,000 barrels per day between 2023 and 2026 -- this would be quite attractive, and yet, at the same time, the goal seems achievable, considering SU's solid performance so far this year.

Despite turnaround activity, Suncor Energy generated highly appealing cash flows during the most recent quarter. Operating cash flow stood at CAD$3.83 billion, which was up by more than CAD$1.0 billion on a year-over-year basis. While Suncor Energy reinvests some of that cash in its business, in order to maintain existing assets and to hit its production growth goals, there is ample cash left over for other purposes. After accounting for capital expenditures of CAD$2.05 billion, Suncor Energy had around CAD$1.8 billion of free cash flow left over to pay for shareholder returns and to reduce debt levels.

During the most recent quarter, Suncor Energy used CAD$1.5 billion of its free cash flow for shareholder distributions, with a little more than half of that going to dividends, while a little less than half of that (CAD$700 million) was paid out via dividends. At current prices, this makes for a dividend yield of around 4.2%, while the buyback yield is around 5% per year. Reducing the share count by 5% per year will result in a huge boost to per-share metrics such as earnings per share and cash flow per share. For perspective, Apple's (AAPL) famed buybacks are currently running at a pace of around 3% per year, meaning Suncor Energy's buybacks are way more effective.

So far this year, Suncor Energy has reduced its net debt by CAD$800 million, bringing the net debt level to CAD$9.05 billion as of the end of the second quarter. Suncor's goal is to reduce net debt to CAD$8.0 billion. Based on the H1 net debt reduction pace, Suncor Energy could hit this goal in the first quarter of 2025, but we might even see the goal being hit during the current quarter, Q4, thanks to higher production in H2, which should be positive for cash flows. But even in a scenario where it takes a little longer, Suncor Energy will hit its net debt target in the foreseeable future. At that point, the company will not reduce its net debt further, and will instead amp up its shareholder returns further (now at 75% of free cash flow). This will most likely happen via more buybacks, especially at the current undemanding valuation. Investors got a nice shareholder yield from Suncor Energy in H1, but things will improve further -- from a shareholder payout perspective, the best is thus yet to come.

Is Suncor Energy A Good Investment?

Like all other energy companies, Suncor Energy is dependent on oil prices to some degree. While the company generated positive cash flows even in 2020, cash flows were much lower back then -- if oil prices were to crash, Suncor Energy's results would be weaker than today. Lower oil prices are thus a risk, and with a slowdown in China and a potential recession in the US, there are macro issues that might cause lower oil prices in the future. That being said, there are also factors that could result in higher oil prices, and even if oil prices were to pull back, I do not believe that we would see COVID-level oil prices. After all, that was much more than a normal recession due to lockdowns, broad travel restrictions, and so on.

SU is not risk-free, but there are many things to like here: Production is growing nicely, debt is being paid down, shareholder returns are strong and will improve further, and Suncor Energy also is quite inexpensive: Based on current forecasts, the company trades at an enterprise value to EBITDA ratio of just 4.8, which is very undemanding.

Likewise, the free cash flow multiple is pretty low as well -- Suncor trades at CAD$67 billion right now, while free cash flows over the last four quarters totaled around CAD$8.5 billion, which makes for an excellent free cash flow yield of 13%.

Overall, I think that Suncor Energy is thus a very attractive pick at current prices for those who want energy exposure and/or a pick with great shareholder returns.

data.title: Ardmore Shipping: Improving Financials, But Nothing Exciting (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Ardmore Shipping (NYSE:ASC) is a product and chemical shipping company specializing in the ownership and operation of small vessels. The ASC fleet consists of 22 owned tankers (18 MR and 4 handy size) and 4 operated vessels through charter agreements.

I have covered Ardmore Shipping previously, issuing an initial buy rating at $13.68 until $22.22 with a short recommendation. Investors should view this as an update to my previous articles on the company, where I provided an extensive business overview. I concluded the last article with this prediction:

Moreover, last year there was a very profitable similar setup when Ardmore Shipping briefly traded above NAV, but in the following months, it lost more than 30%. While I'm not expecting such a decline, I wouldn't go long at this valuation. I could consider initiating a short position to hedge other tanker positions, such as Okeanis (ECO) or Tsakos Energy Navigation (TNP).

Indeed, the company followed the same pattern as last year and since then, the stock has fallen around 25%. As we approach the tankers' strong season, I believe it is time to review Ardmore Shipping again. I still wouldn't consider a long position; however, I believe that a short position is no longer prudent.

Another important factor is that, on July 8, the company announced that the CEO Anthony Gurnee will retire and Gernot Ruppelt, the CCO, will succeed him. It is too early to assess whether this will have positive changes in the future, such as improved shareholder returns.

Product Tanker Overview

As mentioned in previous articles, the product tanker order book has been increasing and currently represents 17.5% of the total fleet. For comparison, one year ago was 9% of the fleet. Despite there being an important need for fleet renewal due to the aging vessels, this huge increase will impact future earnings.

Moreover, in the last few months many crude tankers have shifted to the clean trade, increasing supply and suppressing rates.

On the demand side, product tankers experienced an important boost due to Red Sea disruption. While I don't expect a near term resolution, it will be difficult to see further benefits from this front. Moreover, recently refinery margins have been under pressure leading to Chinese refinery bankruptcies.

Despite expecting that most of the crude tankers transporting clean products will shift back to crude shipments in the coming months, increasing rates during the strong season. The increased order book along with the grimmer demand picture, makes me much less optimistic regarding future rates than a few months ago.

Financial Position & Stock Valuation

Ardmore's financial position is rock solid. However, I consider this to be a poor capital allocation. Considering the receivables and payables, Ardmore Shipping has $18 million in net cash.

A shipping company with owned vessels and net cash is something out of the ordinary. Typically, vessels are leveraged to increase returns and improve profitability. To further aggravate the situation, management did not disclose the company's future plans with such a strong balance sheet, and shareholder returns are modest, with just one-third of earnings as dividends. However, the intention to approach to zero debt could be an advantage in a prolonged bear market, which I do not expect.

Besides the capital allocation, an important factor in valuing the company is to calculate the NAV. I will use the same methodology as in the past, using Arctic asset values and Q2 figures:

Currently, the stock trades at around 0.85 times NAV. This discount is the main reason I won't short the stock anymore; however, I do not see any appeal in going long. I believe that the company is fairly valued at around $17-$18. At current valuations, I would favor the peers who pay out most of their earnings as dividends and have similar valuations. For example, Hafnia (HAFN) trades at a similar valuation and pays out around 80% of its earnings as dividends, while d'Amico (OTCQX:DMCOF) trades at a larger discount and aims to pay out around 50%.

If product tanker rates follow the usual seasonal patterns, they should start to increase this month. Such an increase could generate some momentum and rerate the stock higher. Additionally, although I don't understand why, Ardmore typically trades at a slight premium compared to its peers, which could still justify a slightly higher valuation.

Risk

There are several risks that could affect Ardmore Shipping. At a company-specific level, the main risk is management's desire to grow the fleet. In the past, management has stated growth as one of the main priorities. However, at least until now, they haven't increased the fleet.

At a more general level, I see two main risks. In the short term, the most important risk is a weaker-than-expected strong season, which would impact sentiment and likely push the stock lower. In a longer-term horizon, the main risk is a global recession or a slowdown in growth, which would decrease demand and reduce rates.

Conclusion

Ardmore Shipping has a rock-solid balance sheet. However, it only pays out one-third of earnings to shareholders as dividends and management has not provided a clear explanation of their future plans.

I consider the current valuation fair, and I do not see much potential for either a long or short position. A near-term spike in rates could benefit the stock; however, I see more downside than upside potential given the grimmer outlook after the strong season.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Recent Strategy Should Drive Its Stock Price Higher
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With the stock price currently at half of its peak reached at the end of 2021, Alexandria Real Estate (NYSE:ARE) stock remains disconnected from the company's growing fundamentals, making it undervalued. The recent strategy focusing on property improvements and mega-campuses should continue to drive growth and help the valuation revert to its average.

Recent Strategy

In recent years, Alexandria Real Estate has focused on essential aspects regarding its locations, namely:

Purchasing or transforming them into Class A/A+ properties, which signify superior building quality, constructed within the last 15 years, and located in prime market areas. With these high-quality properties, Alexandria is able to offer tenants some essential features that are hard to find elsewhere, which in turn provides the REIT with the opportunity to raise rent values.

Mega-campuses, a term first used by the company in the 2019 annual report. These campuses span tens or even hundreds of thousands of square meters, containing multiple "clusters." This term refers to the grouping of several organizations or companies with similar plans, ideas, or objectives, where each entity's contribution leads to the discovery of innovative products or the development of existing ones. In the case of Alexandria's mega-campuses, entities from the biotechnology field come together with the goal of advancing or discovering new treatments for human health.

Furthermore, owning high-quality properties, their ongoing development, and focusing on campuses help the upward growth of the business to originate from these specific areas. As long as the strategy remains consistent, I expect this trend to continue its course. All these efforts help Alexandria remain a reliable partner for the companies it collaborates with-these being, in fact, essential in the world we live in, as without constant new discoveries, it would be difficult to combat the increasingly advanced and resistant diseases we face today.

Current Properties Portfolio

Alexandria currently owns a total of 408 properties (as of Q2 2024), with most of them included in the 28 mega-campuses it operates. These campuses are providing an increasing share of revenue, with the REIT aiming to generate over 90% of its rental income from these complexes in the coming years. For now, as of the second quarter of 2024, that percentage stands at around 74%.

The total number of properties has shown a declining trend starting in 2023, which has continued into 2024. The primary reason for this trend lies in a higher volume of properties sold compared to those acquired, suggesting that Alexandria has found fewer acquisition opportunities. However, I do not see this trend as a red flag, but rather as a portfolio optimization process.

California - The Opportunity?

The company's properties are predominantly located within the United States, with the exception of 12 buildings, which are in Canada.

One standout city is Boston, which, although it does not have the largest number of properties, contributes significantly more revenue compared to other locations. Then we have San Diego, California, which, despite having 99 properties with a 95.1% occupancy rate, is expected to generate around $328 million. The key difference between these two regions is that Boston hosts 8 mega-campuses that encompass most of the 73 properties, including Alexandria Center at Kendall Square, which is the largest in terms of revenue generation.

Therefore, the higher number of complexes and their favourable positioning makes the rent per SF significantly higher in Boston compared to San Diego. It's worth noting that the area's average is measured in SF (square foot), while Alexandria reports in RSF (rentable square footage), so the difference is not exact but is good enough to provide an understanding:

  • In Boston, rents have reached approximately $95 per SF, following a consistent year-on-year increase, while the average rent Alexandria charges in this area is around $84.
  • In San Francisco, the average is around $67, which is also reflected in the rates that Alexandria charges for its properties.
  • In San Diego, the average is about $55, which is considerably higher compared to the company's own average in the region of approximately $45.

The discrepancy between Boston and the other markets is quite remarkable, even though the vacancy rate for laboratories in Boston (~20%) is very similar to that in San Francisco (~20%) and significantly higher than in San Diego (~14.3%).

Moreover, the total occupied laboratory space in the Boston area is notably larger than in the other areas.

But why does Boston enjoy such "popularity"? One reason is the presence of the most sought-after, high-quality, and top-ranked university in the United States-Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Students, whether at the bachelor's, master's, or doctoral level, can specialize in a variety of fields, including health, pharmacy, biotechnology, and others. As a result, MIT is one of the most successful universities in terms of training individuals to work in the field of treatment innovation, both in large multinationals and in founding start-ups.

Considering all of this, it is no wonder why Alexandria achieves the highest rental rates in Boston and why this city is so crucial in the biotechnology industry. Even so, the REIT has also focused on the other regions in recent years, which is why between 2019 and 2023, it acquired 33 properties in San Francisco, 47 in San Diego, and 34 in Boston. Currently, the company has temporarily shifted its strategy, with Alexandria slowing down the property acquisition process in favour of renovating, developing, and expanding those already in its portfolio.

Nevertheless, I, personally, consider the San Diego region to be an excellent opportunity for Alexandria, given that it has the lowest vacancy rate for laboratories among the three regions, and the total investment value within the industry is enormous. For instance, the investment sum for just the second quarter of 2024 in San Diego was nearly $2.4 billion, which is very similar to the entire amount for Boston for the first half of 2024.

Location is crucial when it comes to this type of property (Life Sciences), particularly if they can be used as corporate headquarters, as happened with one of the locations in Cambridge, Massachusetts, which became Moderna's (MRNA) new headquarters in 2023, after Alexandria and other contractors completed its development.

I consider it a positive aspect that the REIT focuses on the largest and most important markets in the industry. I see growth potential, especially considering that properties undergoing renovation will become increasingly high quality, which will drive future rent increases, thereby positively impacting revenue generation. Additionally, it is worth mentioning that Alexandria offers its properties below the average market rent, which provides potential for future rent increases.

Main Risk

The average occupancy rate of properties has been declining, which indicates that Alexandria is unable to fully lease out its laboratories, research centers, and office spaces that it owns or has acquired.

This presents a significant risk, as not only are these spaces sitting empty, but the business also has to bear the costs of maintenance, insurance, and associated taxes. As these unleased spaces increase, Alexandria's profitability could come under pressure, potentially impacting its ability to invest in future projects or renovations.

By enhancing the attractiveness of the properties through improvements and focusing on the most promising locations, as Alexandria is already doing, the occupancy rate should improve in the future.

Valuation

I have a checklist of evaluation metrics specifically for REITs, and ARE is among the very few companies that check off every single one.

As for the historical metrics, I compared eight current price multiples with their averages over the last 3, 5, and 10 years. The remaining checkmarks are simply based on maximum thresholds for price multiples that I would like to see in a REIT to consider it undervalued.

The industry-specific checkmarks were earned by comparing ARE with other healthcare REITs and the valuations at which these peers currently stand.

For example, let's look at P/FFO multiple:

ARE CTRE OHI WELL HR DOC VTR
P/FFO TTM 12.97 21.27 14.36 32.39 11.26 12.09 20.69
MEDIAN 14.36 W/O WELL 13.66
AVERAGE 17.86 W/O WELL 15.44

Moreover, if we look at the evolution of FFO (Funds From Operations) in relation to the stock price, we can see that in the past decade, there have been very few occasions where the company has traded below the blue line. The average P/FFO (Price to Funds From Operations) over the last 10 years is around 18.7, while the current P/FFO is 12.4. Even with a conservative margin of safety of only 80% of the average P/FFO-equivalent to a P/FFO of 15.1-the stock price should be trading around $143, considering the principle of reversion to the mean.

It is also noteworthy that the stock price has completely disconnected from the company's fundamentals, which have continued to grow. This trend became more evident after the FED began cutting rates. It's somewhat similar to the situation from 2019-2021, but at that time, it was on the overvaluation side.

Conclusion

It's not unusual for the market to assign a valuation that is somewhat subdued, especially given the current economic situation. However, I believe that in the case of ARE, there is also a bit of overreaction.

Market reactions are often exaggerated during times of economic stress, which can lead to such disconnections. In the case of ARE, the divergence between its intrinsic growth and the declining share price may indicate an opportunity for long-term investors, as eventually, fundamentals tend to reassert themselves and bring valuations back in line, aided by successful execution of its mega-campus strategy.

I will continue to keep an eye on the occupancy rate, but ultimately, I consider ARE a REIT that has every chance to continue its growth in revenues and funds from operations. It also offers a dividend yield of approximately 4.5% at the current price, supported by a payout ratio of only about 70% of AFFO. Therefore, my rating is a Buy.

data.title: Clash Of Titans: Diverging Global And Emerging Market Mid-Year Active Performance
data.description:
By Anu Ganti

Much of recent market commentary has been focused on market concentration concerns,1 thanks to the dominance of mega-cap stocks in the U.S., with the S&P 500® Top 50 outperforming the S&P 500 by 5% for the 12 months ending in September 2024.

But as we've noted in our inaugural SPIVA® Global Mid-Year 2024 Scorecard, concentration trends can manifest at a broader level than individual securities, and more specifically at a country level. The U.S. has significantly outperformed the rest of the world over the past decade, with a 10-year annualized return for the S&P World Index of 10.6%, compared to an equivalent return of 6.2% for the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index. As a result, the weight of the U.S. component of the S&P World Index has risen accordingly, from 47% in June 2009 to over 70% as of the end of September 2024.

Turning our attention to the superpower on the other side of the globe, China's weight in our emerging market benchmark, the S&P/IFCI Composite, declined from a peak of 37% in 2020 to 24% in June 2024 given the market's economic and real estate woes. Since then, its weight rebounded to 27% as of September 2024, as China's market staged a dramatic rally, with the S&P China BMI outperforming the S&P Emerging Ex-China BMI by 8% YTD through September.

So how did global equity active managers fare in this environment of contrasting U.S. and China performances in the first half of 2024? Our report shows that 71% of U.S.-domiciled Global Equity funds underperformed the S&P World Index, consistent with what we would expect from funds that systematically underweight the U.S. Meanwhile, international managers who gained outside-benchmark U.S. exposures may have benefited, as only 56% of U.S.-based international funds underperformed the S&P World Ex-U.S. Index.

Exhibit 2 shows that underperformance rates for the Global Equity (USD) category historically tended to worsen with U.S. outperformance. For global funds domiciled in Europe, Japan, Canada and Australia, the results were even bleaker, with 72%-82% of funds underperforming, perhaps because they were even more underweight the U.S. than their U.S.-domiciled counterparts. Underweighting the U.S. by these managers could potentially be due to home bias tendencies2 and a greater proclivity for domestic exposures.

Conversely, country dynamics were likely more accommodating for emerging market equity managers. Despite stock-level concentration headwinds consistent with those observed in the U.S., emerging market managers who were underweight to China may have benefited from the country's poor performance. The S&P Emerging BMI underperformed the S&P Emerging Ex-China BMI by 2.5% in H1 2024.

In addition, Exhibit 3 illustrates that managers who tilted outside of their emerging market universe into developed markets, including the outperforming U.S. market, could have benefited considerably, as the S&P Developed BMI outperformed the S&P Emerging BMI by 2.6% over the same period. Perhaps capitalizing on both of these tailwinds, the U.S. Emerging Market Funds category posted majority outperformance, with 54% outperforming the benchmark S&P/IFCI Composite. The European-domiciled emerging market category fared slightly worse, with 55% of funds underperforming the benchmark.

Overall, the stellar performance of the U.S. compared to the rest of the world, coupled with China's underperformance, may have led to mixed results across the Global Equity and Emerging Market Equity active fund categories during the first half of 2024. Although China's rally has pulled back recently, if its turnaround is sustained, the tailwinds from an underweight to China may turn into potential headwinds for emerging market managers, the results of which we will have to wait to uncover once our year-end scorecards are released. Until then, dig deeper into how active funds across our reported categories and across regions fared in our SPIVA Global Mid-Year 2024 Scorecard.

1 Iacurci, Greg, "Is the U.S. stock market too 'concentrated'? Here's what to know," CNBC, July 1, 2024.

2 Issifu, Sherifa, "Connecting the S&P/ASX 200 to U.S. Equity Icons," S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, June 2023.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2024 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NIO: Not For The Faint-Hearted: Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Investment thesis

I’m initiating NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) stock with a buy for mid- to long-term investors. I expect to see an upside surprise for NIO in 2025 as the company, among other Chinese players, finds a stable footing in Beijing’s stimulus plan, which should boost consumer spending and confidence. NIO can be considered a premium EV brand with a focus on battery tech, and that can be seen in the company’s focus on the premium segment of the market, as well as heightening the user experience through NIO Houses and NIO Power. NIO has two main selling points, in my opinion. The first is the leeway it gives customers through innovations such as its Battery-as-a-Service (BaaS) offering, which supports battery-swapping technology. The second is the company’s historically close ties with the Chinese government (which further establish NIO’s roots domestically) balanced with its international expansion in Europe and, more recently, MENA. NIO currently has around 43 battery swap stations in Europe and 2,420 in China, not to mention a more comfortable variety of models for consumers, with eight 2024 models under the theme “lead the change.”

Probably the third thing you need to know about NIO after understanding its innovative position in EV and BaaS and its background with the Chinese government is the fact that NIO isn’t one for the faint-hearted. The stock is underperforming the S&P500 year to date, down 33.8% against the S&P500, up 22.7%. This means the stock is underperforming the S&P500 by 56.5%. NIO is also underperforming its domestic peer group, with Li Auto (LI) down 27.2%, XPeng (XPEV) down 17.6%, and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) up 34% over the same period. I think there’s more upside ahead for the stock into 2025 as the pace of outperformance picks up.

If you look at the three-month chart, NIO is showing signs of recovery. As seen below, at the three-month mark, NIO is up 22.3%, above the S&P500 at 4.2%, and its biggest domestic competitor, BYD, is up 17.6%. I'm a buy on both stocks as each company caters to different parts of the EV market, NIO the high end while BYD handles more of the mid and low end.

BYD, for example, is more focused on being cost-friendly. NIO has a similar approach in terms of affordability, which is an approach most EVs gravitated towards after the price wars began. NIO’s edge, however, comes from what it provides with that more premium stamp, which includes BaaS and battery-swapping innovations and, lately, the more cost-friendly Onvo sub-brand. The company gives the customer more flexibility in terms of finances and the amount they’re paying upfront. I believe this approach gives NIO a broader customer base and positions it to compete with both ends of the market: XPeng, for example, caters to the lower end of the market, and Tesla is more focused on high-end customers looking for luxury.

NIO and the Chinese EV market

China EVs growth: NEV penetration

According to the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers, NEV sales were at a record high of 1,287,000 units in September, up from last December’s record of 1,191,000, marking a 42.3% year-over-year increase and a 17% increase from August. The sales include both those in China and exports; NEVs include battery electric vehicles, plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, and fuel cell vehicles. Excluding exports, domestic NEV sales were 1,176,000 units in September, exceeding the record of 1,080,000 units in December last year and by 45.5% year over year and 18.8% from August, as seen in the graph below. There is momentum within China for NEVs, even before economic recovery. Part of my optimism about NIO is attributed to the soon-to-improve consumer spending power in China, which should allow for the momentum of July through September to extend into next year.

NIO in China:

I don’t think we’re strangers to the fact that NIO is the Chinese government’s best buddy (or one of them, let’s say), but at a price. Back in 2020, when the company was going under due to the COVID-19 pandemic sinking car sales in China, NIO went into an agreement with the municipal government of Hefei, the capital of China’s Anhui province, and got a bailout of over 1.4 billion injection into a subsidiary in China created by NIO and called “NIO China.” In return, the company established a new headquarters in the city and decided to expand its operations and “deepen its relationship with local ecosystem partners in Hefei.” The deal, finalized in April, also took a bite out of NIO’s own pocket, with the company pumping $600 million into NIO China. Other strings attached included NIO’s commitment to its core businesses in China, such as NIO power, vehicle R&D, etc.

Last month, the company announced that its NIO China received a new investment and got into definitive agreements with Hefei Jianheng New Energy Automobile Investment Fund Partnership, Anhui Provincial Emerging Industry Investment Co Ltd, and CS Capital Co Ltd. According to a statement, these investors are expected to invest $470 million in cash. This goes to say that NIO’s chumminess with the government is open-ended in terms of the benefits it can provide.

NIO in history:

Now, for a trip down memory lane, the company was founded in 2014 and is based in Shanghai, China. The company’s mission was to “shape a joyful lifestyle by offering high-performance smart electric vehicles and being the best user enterprise.” The company then started delivering vehicles in mid-2018 and became publicly traded in the US in 2018 and announced that its “cash balance is not adequate to provide the required working capital and liquidity for continuous operation in the next 12 months,” triggering the 2020 bailout. It’s no secret that NIO benefits from the Chinese government subsidies and tax incentives for EV manufacturers, but it is one among many others, such as BYD, Li Auto, and XPeng.

I think some might argue that “government handouts” won’t be sustainable in the long term. However, I think the Chinese government is on a mission, and it is implementing strategic government policies, financial incentives and subsidies, and infrastructure development to reach its goals. After the COVID-19 slowdown, NIO started diversifying its legs of growth and opted for the battery swapping stations in Europe to offset the former. I think this diversification strategy works in its favor as it has several eggs in different baskets.

NIO’s sales:

NIO’s leading brand currently has eight models, of which five are SUVs and three are sedans. The company also unveiled its ET9 last December and will start delivering the vehicles in the first quarter of 2025. I expect the latter to boost sales with the recent China Stimulus plan. As for NIO’s deliveries, the company had another impressive month, September, and deliveries during the month marked the fifth consecutive month where NIO delivered over 20,000 units. According to a news release on October 1, the company said the following:

  • NIO delivered 21,181 vehicles in September, 20,349 of which were under the main NIO brand and 832 under the Onvo sub-brand (Onvo is a lower-cost brand that NIO launched in mid-May this year.) This means deliveries increased by over 35% year over year, as seen below.

  • ES6 and ET5T delivered 13,491 vehicles during the month, making up 63.69% of NIO’s deliveries and 66.3% of the NIO brand. The ES6 delivered 55,614 vehicles, making up 37.2% of NIO deliveries, and the ET5T delivered 36,641 vehicles, making up 24.5% of deliveries.

  • Onvo launched the L60 model on September 19, and just under ten days later, on September 28, it started deliveries, which made up around 4% of NIO’s deliveries in September.

Three days ago, the company announced it had reached the 600,000th production vehicle milestone, only four months after delivering its 500,000. I think these wins should be taken for what they are: the company’s efforts in innovation.

Valuation

NIO currently has a market cap of $12.17 billion and an enterprise value of $12.38 billion. Its beta is 1.86, higher than the market average. NIO is down 31.64% in the last 52 weeks. And I’m going to be honest: NIO is cheap, but it isn’t a stock for investors looking to make money on the spot and cash out, and it is definitely not for the faint-hearted. The best we can do is give an educated guess based on the company’s future prospects of growth, and in NIO’s case, these are plenty.

The company is currently investing in R&D and focused on market expansions, which explains its low profitability. According to the company’s 2Q24 earnings report, R&D was up 12% quarter over quarter, and gross profit was up 1,841% year over year and 246.3% from 1Q24. Gross margin also increased significantly, going up to 9.7% from 1% in a year ago and 4.9% from 1Q24. Loss from operations was down 14.2% year over year and 3.4% quarter over quarter, and net loss was down 16.7% year over year and 2.7% quarter over quarter. According to the company’s CFO, Stanley Yu Qu, the vehicle gross margin was up 12.2% in 2Q24 due to the ongoing cost optimizations, noting: “We will continue to focus on efficient R&D and infrastructure investment, leverage the growth potential in the mass market, adopt flexible market strategies, and continuously optimize our product portfolio. We are confident that these efforts will result in steady improvements in gross profit and cost efficiency in the future.”

The market sentiment on the stock is positive. Over 17% of Street Analysts, according to data from Refinitiv, are a strong buy on the stock, and over 35% are a buy. Around 43% of Street Analysts are a hold, and only 3.6% are a sell, with 0% strong sells. I think the market is on the fence about NIO’s growth potential and when it’ll materialize.

The median PT and mean PT have had a downward trend since July, and this is to be expected with a stock with such high price volatility. The former was at $7.7 in mid-July and went down to $7.3 in August. It continued going down to $6.1 in September but has recently rebounded slightly to $7. As for the mean PT, it was at $8.45 in July, $8.2 in August, $7.5 in September and $7.43 currently.

In comparison to its domestic peers, NIO seems to be relatively cheap at current levels. With EV/Sales ratio (TTM) at 1.42 and EV/Sales at 1.24, NIO trades slightly higher than Li Auto with EV/Sales (TTM) t 0.91 and EV/Sales at 0.81 and BYD with EV/Sales (TTM) at 1.31 and EV/Sales 1.12. Also, NIO is cheaper than XPeng with EV/Sales (TTM) at 2 and EV/Sales at 1.71, and Tesla with EV/Sales (TTM) at 7.1 and EV/Sales at 6.8.

What’s next: Globalization efforts

I believe that through its continued technological advancements and the cutting-edge technology that its vehicles incorporate, NIO is positioned to snatch more than “40% of the market share in the battery electric vehicle segment, which is priced above RMB 300,000 in China.” On that note, earlier this month, NIO collaborated with CYVN Holdings based in Abu Dhabi, an investor in smart & advanced technology, to enter the MENA and launch NIO MENA (Middle East and North Africa). The agreement was signed on October 4th with the presidents of the UAE and Egypt present. According to NIO’s founder, chairman, and CEO, William Li, this is a vital step towards the company’s globalization process.

“Our collaboration with CYVN Holdings is a cornerstone in our strategy to enhance global accessibility to smart electric vehicles and push the boundaries of technological innovation.”

The UAE will be the initial market for this collaboration as a key player in deploying both 1. advanced autonomous driving systems and 2. battery-swapping technologies. This partnership, as previously mentioned, isn’t just for globalization purposes. Both NIO and CYVN are planning to establish a “state-of-the-art” research and development centre in the UAE’s capital, Abu Dhabi, to focus on autonomous driving and AI advancements. Egypt will have a hand in this, too, and NIO MENA will work with local partners in Egypt. I see a lot more opportunities for NIO to boost its sales and, alongside it, its market share in China and abroad.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lexicon Faces Uphill Battle As Inpefa Launch Disappoints, Eyes Type 1 Diabetes
data.description:
Relative to the broader market and despite its higher risk profile, Lexicon Pharmaceuticals' (NASDAQ:LXRX) stock is unchanged since my January analysis and "sell" recommendation. Back then, the focus was on Lexicon's heart failure drug, Inpefa (sotagliflozin). I noted that Inpefa, FDA-approved in May 2023, faced competition from other SGLT2 inhibitors like dapagliflozin (Farxiga) or empagliflozin (Jardiance), marketed by much larger companies like Eli Lilly (LLY).

Treatment recommendations I frequent (e.g., UpToDate) cite dapagliflozin or empagliflozin as the preferred SGLT2 inhibitors for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). In fact, sotagliflozin is marked as an "other SGLT2 inhibitor," with uncertainty regarding its impact on HFrEF in patients without diabetes. This is an important notation, as many heart failure patients have diabetes. Per my research, it's pretty clear that there is no medical reason for a prescriber to utilize sotagliflozin ahead of the more common SGLT2 inhibitors. Given that Lexicon is a much smaller company than Eli Lilly and others, this makes for a very challenging drug launch. Let's see how the drug is doing so far on the market.

In Q2, Inpefa revenue totaled $1.6 million. Lexicon noted that its "launch in heart failure continues to progress and market access discussions remain ongoing." SG&A costs increased from $30 million in Q2 '23 to $39.2 million. That's not too bad. R&D expenses increased to $17.6 million. Lexicon's net loss for Q2 widened to $53.4 million.

So, Inpefa's launch is going just as I expected: not good. It's positive that the company isn't pouring too much SG&A investments into this indication. I think it is, clearly, a negative NPV project. Interestingly, days after Q2 earnings, Lexicon announced their intention to cut half of their workforce to "optimize its promotional efforts for INPEFA® (sotagliflozin) in heart failure and reallocate resources to effectively support the potential commercial launch of ZYNQUISTA™ (sotagliflozin) for improvement of glycemic control in adults with type 1 diabetes and chronic kidney disease (CKD)." This sounds like they are, wisely, waving the white flag in HF.

So Lexicon is pivoting back to this indication after a tough go in heart failure. This isn't the first time sotagliflozin has flirted with type 1 diabetes (T1D). Back in 2019, sotagliflozin received a CRL from the FDA after a mixed AdComm review "expressing concerns about an increased risk for diabetic ketoacidosis," a major and life-threatening complication of T1D. The NDA was resubmitted in June, and the FDA assigned a PDUFA review date of December 20, 2024. However, another AdComm will be held on October 31 before the FDA makes its decision. If approved, sotagliflozin would be the first SGLT2 inhibitor to receive FDA approval for this indication, though I am not aware of any others desiring this indication in the U.S. The consensus is that SGLT2 inhibitors should only be utilized in T1D in rare cases and with "extreme caution." Although the U.S. has been resistant thus far, Europe has approved SGLT2 inhibitors for T1D, so there is some precedent from regulatory authorities.

Lexicon is also testing sotagliflozin in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). This is, in my opinion, a wild card. HCM is structurally distinct from HF.
